- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
  Across four decades of studies, intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially on complex or creative tasks. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence supports weighting interesting work heavily but not almost exclusively.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)**  
  Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, particularly when they feel controlling. The result is a warning against letting pay/status dominate, not evidence that pay and career structure never matter.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020)**  
  Fit between a person’s vocational interests and their work is reliably associated with job satisfaction. The association is meaningful but not overwhelming, implying interest is important but not the sole determinant of good career outcomes.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
  Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support all relate to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. “Interesting work” matters, but leadership, autonomy, impact, team quality, and work context matter too.

- **“Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
  The job characteristics model argues that motivating work depends on skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. A role can be less intellectually exciting yet still motivating if it offers autonomy, ownership, and visible impact.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
  Person–job and person–organization fit predict job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The evidence favors choosing the environment that fits your values, skills, and preferred trajectory, rather than optimizing only for task interestingness.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
  Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This supports not overweighting a somewhat higher salary when the work itself, fit, and growth path differ substantially.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)**  
  People tend to overestimate how much income affects happiness when income is made salient. This is strong evidence against letting a salary bump dominate a career decision.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises With Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth (2021)**  
  Moment-to-moment well-being continues to rise with income, including at higher income levels. The relationship is roughly logarithmic, so additional income can matter but often has diminishing practical salience for already-high earners.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction. However, “calling” is not just interesting tasks; it also involves identity, meaning, contribution, and how the person frames the work.

- **“The Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
  Meaningful work arises from authenticity, self-efficacy, self-esteem, purpose, belonging, and transcendence. Intellectual interest is one path to meaning, but mentoring, leadership, mission, and impact can also create it.

- **“Follow Your Heart or Your Head? A Longitudinal Study of the Facilitating Role of Calling and Ability in the Pursuit of a Challenging Career” — Dobrow Riza & Heller (2015)**  
  Calling and ability both shape persistence in demanding careers. Passion helps, but the paper cautions against treating passion or interest as sufficient without considering capability, opportunity, and long-term career structure.

- **“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018)**  
  People who believe interests are fixed are less open to new domains and more likely to disengage when difficulty arises. This argues against judging a role only by how interesting it seems immediately, because interest can sometimes develop through mastery and ownership.

- **“Understanding Contemporary Forms of Exploitation: Attributions of Passion Serve to Legitimize the Poor Treatment of Workers” — Kim, Campbell, Shepherd & Kay (2020)**  
  Observers view passionate workers as more legitimately asked to accept unpaid, unpleasant, or excessive work. This is a caution that loving the work can make people undervalue compensation, boundaries, and bargaining power.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile (1997)**  
  Intrinsic motivation is central to creativity, especially in knowledge work and research-like tasks. But extrinsic factors such as resources, autonomy, recognition, and supportive leadership can either undermine or amplify intrinsic motivation depending on how they are structured.

answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath, 1999**  
  People systematically overestimate how much others are motivated by pay and other extrinsic rewards, while underestimating intrinsic motives. This supports the idea that “interesting work” is often undervalued, though it does not imply it should be the only factor.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**  
  Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. The evidence suggests pay and status can sometimes crowd out enjoyment, but the effects depend on reward type and context.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci, 2005**  
  Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of motivation and well-being at work. This favors roles with intrinsically engaging work and real ownership, but also highlights team context and support.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**  
  Job characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, feedback, and task significance strongly predict satisfaction and motivation. Interesting work matters a lot, but social and contextual features of the role also matter.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris, 1987**  
  Jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are associated with higher motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing rich, engaging work over narrower extrinsic upgrades.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The relevant question is not only “which work is more interesting,” but where your abilities, values, and preferred environment fit best.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**  
  Pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This weakens a salary-first argument, especially when both options are already highly paid.

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life But Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**  
  Higher income improves life evaluation, but in their data day-to-day emotional well-being flattened above a certain income level. This suggests that after financial comfort, daily work experience may dominate marginal salary increases.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021**  
  Experience-sampling data found that well-being continues to rise with income, contradicting a simple income plateau. This means salary should not be dismissed, though its marginal value may still be smaller than work quality for many people.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**  
  This paper reconciles prior findings by showing that well-being generally rises with income, while an unhappy minority may plateau. The evidence argues against both extremes: neither ignore salary nor optimize almost exclusively for it.

- **“Immediate Rewards Predict Adherence to Long-Term Goals” — Woolley & Fishbach, 2017**  
  Immediate enjoyment predicts persistence better than distant outcomes across several goal domains. This supports heavily weighting the day-to-day interestingness of the work, because it affects sustained motivation.

- **“Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model” — Sheldon & Elliot, 1999**  
  Goals aligned with a person’s interests and values lead to more effort, greater attainment, and higher well-being. This supports choosing work that feels personally meaningful rather than merely instrumentally attractive.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. Interesting and meaningful work can therefore have large subjective value beyond compensation.

- **“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010**  
  Meaningful work arises from autonomy, authenticity, competence, purpose, belonging, and impact on others. This implies that leading a team could be meaningful even if the technical work is less interesting.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015**  
  Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, partly through teaching and coordination. A management role can create leverage and impact, but the value depends on whether you would actually enjoy and be good at that work.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li & Shue, 2019**  
  High individual-contributor performance often predicts promotion into management, but not necessarily managerial success. This cautions against treating a team-lead role as automatically better unless management itself is a good fit.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006**  
  People tend to overestimate the happiness impact of income when focusing on income during a decision. This is relevant because salary and title are salient, while everyday task enjoyment is easier to underweight.

- **“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want” — Wilson & Gilbert, 2005**  
  People often mispredict how future events will affect their happiness because they focus too much on salient differences and too little on adaptation and daily life. This applies directly to career choices where salary, prestige, team leadership, and task interest may be misweighted.
